policy_statement: The Government of Armenia approved the Nationally Determined Contributions (2021-2030) with an unconditional target to reduce emissions by 40% below 1990 levels by 2030.
mechanism_description: The government approved an NDC that sets an unconditional national mitigation target, creating an economy-wide pathway to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Policy target-setting + governance → cross-sector decarbonization actions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city adopts a binding or guiding community-wide emissions target (e.g., % reduction by a year, net-zero), aligning planning, budgeting, regulations, and investments across energy, buildings, transport, waste, and industry.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of fossil fuels, improved energy efficiency, fuel switching/electrification, methane reductions, and other sector measures lower the mass flow of GHGs released to the atmosphere.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower community-wide GHG emissions (CO₂e) leading to reduced cumulative atmospheric GHG concentrations and reduced radiative forcing over time.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is achieving economy-wide reductions in greenhouse gas emissions (often to net-zero) across major sectors.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s central function is to set an economy-wide emissions outcome that triggers and coordinates decarbonization measures, yielding ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While targets can indirectly improve efficiency or resilience, their defining and largest intended climate effect is emissions mitigation at the community scale, not adaptation (hazard/exposure reduction), not resource efficiency as an end in itself, and not ecosystem-based carbon sequestration as the primary lever.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some targets include “net-zero” via offsets or land-sector sinks; even then, the dominant climate-relevant purpose remains achieving net emissions reductions/neutrality (mitigation). If a document is purely aspirational with no implementation linkage, it is still categorized by intended dominant pathway (mitigation).  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that sets a community-wide GHG reduction or net-zero target shares the same primary mechanism—economy-wide emissions reduction—regardless of sector mix or local context.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether delivery relies more on efficiency, electrification, renewables, waste methane controls, offsets, or nature-based sinks requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Targets framed mainly as “climate neutrality” can be met with offsets or external credits; classification remains Mitigation, but downstream interpretation should note whether reductions are in-boundary vs. compensated. Targets limited to municipal operations (not community-wide) would be a different mechanism and should not be conflated.
key_indicators: "approved Armenia's Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC)"; "tenyear implementation period (2021-2030)"; "unconditional mitigation target"; "40% reduction below 1990 emissions levels"; "to be achieved in 2030"
instance_edge_case_notes: NDCs can be treated as governance/planning frameworks, but the text is dominated by the quantified emissions goal; classification as target-setting is most appropriate.

policy_statement: Armenia plans to construct about 1000 MW of solar power plants (including autonomous ones) by 2030 to support reaching solar generation targets.
mechanism_description: The plan is to construct solar power plants to increase solar electricity generation, which displaces fossil-fueled generation and lowers power-sector emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Renewable electricity deployment → fossil generation displacement → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Electricity supply mix shifts as PV capacity and generation increase (utility-scale, rooftop, or community solar), changing dispatch and reducing fossil plant run-hours and output.  
2) Mediating process: PV produces electricity without on-site combustion; on the grid, this zero/low-operational-emissions generation substitutes for marginal fossil generation (often gas or coal), reducing fossil fuel use and associated CO2 (and some CH4/N2O) emissions; effects depend on marginal emissions factors, curtailment, and storage/transmission constraints.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in power-sector GHG emissions (tCO2e) and, secondarily, reductions in co-pollutants from fossil generation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is that added solar PV generation displaces fossil electricity generation, lowering power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: PV deployment increases renewable generation that substitutes for fossil generation, yielding a direct decrease in combustion-related GHG emissions—this is classic emissions mitigation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While PV can improve resource efficiency (less fuel input per kWh) and can support resilience if paired with storage/islanding, the principal and most general effect across all such policies is emissions reduction via fossil displacement.  
3) Edge cases applied: If PV is added in a grid with high curtailment or already near-zero marginal emissions, emissions benefits may be smaller, but the intended and typical dominant pathway remains fossil displacement and mitigation. Lifecycle/manufacturing emissions do not overturn the primary operational mitigation effect in standard accounting.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that materially deploys solar PV to increase electricity production shares this dominant mitigation pathway because the mechanism explicitly relies on renewable generation displacing fossil generation.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any further subtype/secondary tagging (e.g., rooftop vs utility-scale, community ownership, resilience microgrids, equity components) is deferred to policy-level evidence and does not change the primary category.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions impact depends on the marginal grid mix, curtailment, and whether PV is paired with storage; PV deployed primarily for backup power/resilience could introduce an adaptation co-benefit, but the canonical mechanism specified is fossil generation displacement (mitigation).
key_indicators: "planned to increase the share of solar energy generation… to at least 15% or 1.8 billion kWh by 2030"; "solar power plants with the capacity of about 1000 MW… are planned to be constructed"
instance_edge_case_notes: Although it mentions constructing solar plants (which could resemble an infrastructure investment), the excerpt does not specify a budget, financing vehicle, or implementing project/program—so it is treated as target-setting with a planned buildout rather than a concrete investment instrument.

policy_statement: Armenia plans to commission the Masrik-1 utility-scale solar PV plant (55 MW peak) by 2022 and hold tenders for additional solar PV plants totaling about 520 MW (including two 200 MW plants).
mechanism_description: The government plans to commission and tender new utility-scale solar PV plants, increasing renewable generation and displacing fossil electricity generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Renewable electricity deployment → fossil generation displacement → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:

2) Mediating process: PV produces electricity without on-site combustion; on the grid, this zero/low-operational-emissions generation substitutes for marginal fossil generation (often gas or coal), reducing fossil fuel use and associated CO2 (and some CH4/N2O) emissions; effects depend on marginal emissions factors, curtailment, and storage/transmission constraints.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in power-sector GHG emissions (tCO2e) and, secondarily, reductions in co-pollutants from fossil generation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is that added solar PV generation displaces fossil electricity generation, lowering power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: PV deployment increases renewable generation that substitutes for fossil generation, yielding a direct decrease in combustion-related GHG emissions—this is classic emissions mitigation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While PV can improve resource efficiency (less fuel input per kWh) and can support resilience if paired with storage/islanding, the principal and most general effect across all such policies is emissions reduction via fossil displacement.  
3) Edge cases applied: If PV is added in a grid with high curtailment or already near-zero marginal emissions, emissions benefits may be smaller, but the intended and typical dominant pathway remains fossil displacement and mitigation. Lifecycle/manufacturing emissions do not overturn the primary operational mitigation effect in standard accounting.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that materially deploys solar PV to increase electricity production shares this dominant mitigation pathway because the mechanism explicitly relies on renewable generation displacing fossil generation.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any further subtype/secondary tagging (e.g., rooftop vs utility-scale, community ownership, resilience microgrids, equity components) is deferred to policy-level evidence and does not change the primary category.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions impact depends on the marginal grid mix, curtailment, and whether PV is paired with storage; PV deployed primarily for backup power/resilience could introduce an adaptation co-benefit, but the canonical mechanism specified is fossil generation displacement (mitigation).
key_indicators: "commission Masrik-1 utility scale solar PV"; "new tenders will be held"; "construction of another 7 solar PVs"; "total installed capacity of about 520 MW"
co_benefits: Potential air quality improvements and energy security benefits from reduced fossil fuel use (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Tendering could be framed as an incentive-program or planning-strategy in some contexts, but the explicit use of "tenders" to contract construction most closely matches procurement-requirement.

policy_statement: Armenia aims to reduce electricity distribution losses to 6.4% in 2027 under Electric Networks of Armenia's 2016-2027 investment program.
mechanism_description: An investment program to reduce electricity distribution losses lowers the amount of electricity that must be generated to deliver the same end-use consumption, reducing associated power-sector emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same electricity service delivered → ↓ transmission/distribution losses → ↓ electricity generation set → ↓ power-sector GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Utilities/grid operators upgrade and operate distribution networks to cut technical losses (e.g., conductor upgrades, transformer replacement, voltage optimization) and/or non-technical losses (e.g., metering, theft reduction).  
2) Mediating physical process: Lower resistive (I²R) and equipment losses and improved accounting reduce energy dissipated as heat or unbilled consumption on the network, decreasing total energy that must be produced to meet delivered demand.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced generation (especially from fossil units on the margin and/or reduced overall fossil share) leads to lower CO₂e emissions from the electricity supply chain.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoided electricity generation due to lower grid losses, which reduces power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Lower distribution losses reduce required electricity generation for the same delivered consumption, yielding direct GHG reductions from the power sector.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While loss reduction can improve reliability and resilience (an adaptation co-benefit) and can be framed as efficiency, the core climate-relevant endpoint is reduced GHG emissions from avoided generation, which is mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a grid is near-fully renewable, loss reduction yields smaller direct GHG benefits (more an efficiency/reliability outcome), but the mechanism as defined still targets avoided generation and thus emissions reductions where fossil generation exists or on the margin.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that reduces distribution losses necessarily reduces required upstream generation to deliver the same end-use electricity, so the primary mitigation pathway holds across contexts (magnitude varies by grid emissions intensity).  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any further subtype (e.g., “grid efficiency,” “utility modernization,” “loss reduction/technical vs non-technical”) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on specific measures and grid mix evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: In very low-carbon grids, the dominant climate effect may shift toward resource efficiency/reliability rather than large GHG reductions; also, if demand rebounds due to lower losses/costs, net emissions benefits can be partially offset.
key_indicators: “Reduction of distribution losses to 6.4% in 2027”; “ENA 2016-2027 Investment program”
co_benefits: Improved grid reliability/quality of supply; reduced operating costs; potential reduction in local air pollutants via lower fossil generation.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as an infrastructure investment program, but the text provided is primarily a quantified loss-reduction target rather than specifying particular capital works.

policy_statement: Armenia plans to reach total small hydropower plant (SHPP) capacity of 430 MW by 2023, supported by the Hydro Energy Development Concept and 15-year Power Purchase Agreements under the Energy Law.
mechanism_description: Armenia supports commissioning new small hydropower plants and 15-year power purchasing agreements to expand renewable electricity supply and displace fossil generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Renewable electricity deployment → fossil generation displacement → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Electricity supply mix shifts as PV capacity and generation increase (utility-scale, rooftop, or community solar), changing dispatch and reducing fossil plant run-hours and output.  
2) Mediating process: PV produces electricity without on-site combustion; on the grid, this zero/low-operational-emissions generation substitutes for marginal fossil generation (often gas or coal), reducing fossil fuel use and associated CO2 (and some CH4/N2O) emissions; effects depend on marginal emissions factors, curtailment, and storage/transmission constraints.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in power-sector GHG emissions (tCO2e) and, secondarily, reductions in co-pollutants from fossil generation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is that added solar PV generation displaces fossil electricity generation, lowering power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: PV deployment increases renewable generation that substitutes for fossil generation, yielding a direct decrease in combustion-related GHG emissions—this is classic emissions mitigation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While PV can improve resource efficiency (less fuel input per kWh) and can support resilience if paired with storage/islanding, the principal and most general effect across all such policies is emissions reduction via fossil displacement.  
3) Edge cases applied: If PV is added in a grid with high curtailment or already near-zero marginal emissions, emissions benefits may be smaller, but the intended and typical dominant pathway remains fossil displacement and mitigation. Lifecycle/manufacturing emissions do not overturn the primary operational mitigation effect in standard accounting.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that materially deploys solar PV to increase electricity production shares this dominant mitigation pathway because the mechanism explicitly relies on renewable generation displacing fossil generation.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any further subtype/secondary tagging (e.g., rooftop vs utility-scale, community ownership, resilience microgrids, equity components) is deferred to policy-level evidence and does not change the primary category.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions impact depends on the marginal grid mix, curtailment, and whether PV is paired with storage; PV deployed primarily for backup power/resilience could introduce an adaptation co-benefit, but the canonical mechanism specified is fossil generation displacement (mitigation).
key_indicators: "Quantitative goal: To reach the total capacity of SHPPs of 430 MW by 2023"; "approved the Hydro Energy Development Concept"; "commissioning of new SHPPs"; "Power Purchasing Agreement for the 15-year period"
instance_edge_case_notes: PPAs are an enabling contractual/market support instrument (often akin to an incentive/guaranteed offtake), but the presence of an explicit MW-by-year goal makes "target-setting" the best single instrument_type here.

policy_statement: Under the Strategy and Action Plan, Armenia will construct medium utility-scale solar PV power plants totaling 175 MW by end-2024, including Masrik-1 (55 MW) in 2022 and additional 120 MW by end-2024.
mechanism_description: Armenia will construct medium utility-scale solar PV power plants to generate electricity, increasing renewable supply and displacing fossil-based power generation.

key_indicators: "will be constructed"; "solar PV power plants"; "total capacity of 175 MW"; "Masrik-1 (55 MW)"; "additional 120 MW by end-2024"; "annually produced electricity"
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollutants from displaced fossil generation; energy security/diversification
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a "target-setting" commitment (175 MW by 2024), but the text emphasizes actual construction of plants, so classified as infrastructure investment.

key_indicators: "construction of 2 Solar PVs"; "capacity of 200MW each"; "envisaged in 2023 and 2024"; "Request for Qualification"; "seeking private developers"; "design, finance, build, own, and operate"; "grid-connected 200MW solar PV power plant"; "'Ayg-1'"
co_benefits: Energy security/reduced fuel imports; local air pollution reduction; job creation and private investment mobilization.
instance_edge_case_notes: The RFQ language also resembles a procurement/PPP-style instrument; however, the core measure described is deployment via building new generation capacity, so it is classified as infrastructure investment.

policy_statement: Armenia will support installation of solar PV plants up to 5 MW through tariff policy and power purchase agreements, targeting 315 MW of such PV capacity by 2030.
mechanism_description: The strategy supports building solar PV plants using tariff policy and power purchase agreements, increasing renewable electricity supply and displacing fossil-based generation.

key_indicators: “supported through tariff policy”; “power purchase agreement”; “construction of solar PV power plants”; “quantitative goal … 315 MW … by 2030”; “installed capacity … up to 5MW”
